scope_note: QA Nuclear Magic Orbit Cert family [280]. Primary sources: (1) Mayer, M. G. (1949). On Closed Shells in Nuclei. Physical Review, 75(12), 1969-1970. DOI: 10.1103/PhysRev.75.1969. (2) Haxel, O., Jensen, J. H. D., & Suess, H. E. (1949). On the 'Magic Numbers' in Nuclear Structure. Physical Review, 75(11), 1766. DOI: 10.1103/PhysRev.75.1766.2. Mechanism: QA Orbit Access Theorem cert [279] (Wall 1960, Pisano period framing). CLAIM (narrow, falsifiable): The canonical nuclear magic numbers {2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126, 184} partition under mod-9 route enumeration (all (b,e) with b+2e=a, b>=1, e>=1, A1-reduced via bq=((b-1)%9)+1, eq=((e-1)%9)+1, orbit_family on (bq,eq,9)) into exactly three classes: (A) a=2 has zero valid routes (b+2e=2 with b>=1, e>=1 is infeasible); (B) {8,20,28,50,82,184} are coprime to 3, yielding pure Cosmos, zero Satellite, zero Singularity; (C) a=126=14*9 is divisible by 9, yielding 62 total routes: 42 Cosmos, 14 Satellite, 6 Singularity. a=126 is the unique magic number in the set divisible by 3. Cert does NOT claim: QA explains nuclear shell closure; orbit class predicts binding energy, angular momentum, or stability; a=184 is a confirmed stable shell (it is a predicted island-of-stability candidate); any physical mechanism connecting orbit access to nuclear physics. Mechanism cert [279] is the algebraic grounding; this cert is the domain application. Checks NMO_1/NMO_2/NMO_3/NMO_4/SRC/F; 6 PASS + 4 FAIL fixtures; self-test ok.
